ORDER ORDER DENYING DEFENDANT'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

LAW AND MOTION 302, THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS DENIED. DEFENDANT FAILS TO PROVIDE A SATISFACTORY EXPLANATION WHY THESE DEFENSES WERE NOT RAISED IN THE FIRST MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. MOTION ALSO DENIED ON ITS MERITS. TO DETERMINE WHETHER OR NOT PLAINTIFF CAN SAFELY PERFORM HIS JOB, THE COURT MUST DETERMINE THE ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS OF HIS JOB. AS THE COURT PREVIOUSLY RULED BASED ON CRIPE V. CITY OF SAN JOSE 261 F. 3D 877, THERE IS A TRIABLE ISSUE OF FACT REGARDING THE ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS OF PLAINTIFF'S JOB. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: CHARLOTTE WALTER WOOLARD, REPORTER: KENT GUBBINE, CSR #5797

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Aug-23-2010 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Erik Rapoport of the Office of the City Attorney (554.3650) for defendant and moving party; John F. Henning III (255.7675) and Lawrence D. Murray (673.0555) for plaintiff and responding party.

Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is denied. Defendant fails to provide a satisfactory explanation why these defenses were not raised in the first motion for summary judgment. Motion also denied on its merits. To determine whether or not plaintiff can safely perform his job, the court must determine the essential functions of his job. As the court previously ruled based on Cripe v. City of San Jose 261 F. 3d 877, there is a triable issue of fact regarding the essential functions of plaintiff's job. Order signed in open court. (302/CWW) Judge: Charlotte Walter Woolard, Reporter: Kent Gubbine, CSR #5797
